formatDefinitions.displayName: Complete with words from the box

exercises.title: A: Word Formation
exercises.instructions: Use the word given in capitals to form a word that fits in the space.
exercises.studentInstruction: Use the word given in capitals to form a word that fits in the space.

items.sentence: A new academic study (1) ...... (HIGH) the (2) ...... (EXTEND) to which student loan debt is shaping career choices among graduates.
Responsibility is (3) ...... (PORTION) unfairly to universities alone, which, critics argue, will go to any (4) ...... (LONG) to recruit international applicants.
items.explanation: Passive PORTION + go to LENGTHS → **apportioned** unfairly; **lengths** to recruit.

items.sentence: (5) ...... (ADD), the findings reveal that many graduates delay major life decisions due to the pressure of outstanding loans.
A controversial funding proposal — which has since been (6) ...... (DRAW) — sought to double tuition fees for all postgraduate programmes.
items.explanation: Funding proposal withdrawn → **withdrawn** (past participle of DRAW).

items.sentence: While the financial burden placed on graduates is not (7) ...... (CONSIDER), it would be misleading to claim that every degree leads to hardship.
items.explanation: Not CONSIDER + double negative → burden is not **inconsiderable**.

items.sentence: The (8) ...... (MAJOR) of respondents admitted they had fallen behind on their (9) ...... (PAY) within the first two years of leaving university.
There will always be a (10) ...... (MINOR) of borrowers who genuinely struggle to manage their finances effectively.
items.explanation: Opposite of majority → a **minority** of borrowers.

exercises.title: B: Key Word Transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence:
Sending those aggressive emails to a client like that is bound to cause you serious problems. **ICE**
You are ....... by sending those aggressive emails to a client like that.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is risky behaviour, so the correct answer is "skating on thin ice".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "skating on thin ice", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
Are you telling me that Rachel gets irritated very easily, Tom? **SHORT**
Are you telling me that Rachel ........ , Tom?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is gets irritated easily, so the correct answer is "has a short temper".

wrongOptions.a short temper: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "has a short temper", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
There’s no doubt that comparing prices online is a worthwhile thing to do. **PAYS**
There’s no doubt ...... prices online.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is worthwhile activity, so the correct answer is "it pays to compare".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "it pays to compare", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
I’d prefer to use every single facility at the spa for the entire afternoon. **WORTH**
I’d rather I ...... by using every single facility at the spa for the entire afternoon.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is get full value, so the correct answer is "got my money's worth".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
I think Marcus is preoccupied with several worries right now. **MIND**
I think Marcus ...... right now.
items.answer: has a lot on his mind
acceptedAnswers: has a lot on his mind

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is preoccupied with worries, so the correct answer is "has a lot on his mind".

wrongOptions.a lot on his mind: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
Why didn’t you look everywhere for your passport? You might have caught the flight. **LOW**
If ...... for your passport, you might have caught the flight.
items.answer: you had searched high and low
acceptedAnswers: you had searched high and low

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is search everywhere, so the correct answer is "you had searched high and low".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
I can’t manage at all with this advanced statistics module, and it worries me. **DEPTH**
I wish ...... this advanced statistics module.
items.answer: I was not out of my depth in
acceptedAnswers: I was not out of my depth in

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is can't cope with difficulty, so the correct answer is "I was not out of my depth in".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "I was not out of my depth in", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
Kevin was the unlucky one so he had to inform Claire that she was being transferred to another branch. **STRAW**
If Kevin ......, he wouldn’t have had to inform Claire that she was being transferred to another branch.
items.answer: had not drawn the short straw
acceptedAnswers: had not drawn the short straw

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is unlucky one chosen, so the correct answer is "had not drawn the short straw".

wrongOptions.not drawn the short straw: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost — use past perfect for the first of two past actions.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "had not drawn the short straw", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

exercises.title: C: Collocations (Financial Terms)
exercises.instructions: Write a word from the box in each gap. There is one word you will not use.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write a word from the box in each gap. There is one word you will not use.

items.sentence: After thirty years, they finally paid off the ...... on their seaside cottage. Now it’s truly theirs!
items.explanation: Loan on a house → paid off the **mortgage**.

items.sentence: All our household bills go out by direct ......, which saves us the hassle of remembering due dates.
items.explanation: Direct debit → bills go out by direct **debit**.

items.sentence: We agreed to buy the furniture in instalments, so we made the first down ...... this afternoon.
Upon retirement, you may choose to receive a single lump ...... or opt for monthly instalments spread over fifteen years.
items.explanation: Lump sum vs monthly → single lump **sum**.

items.sentence: After two decades as a nurse, she now receives a generous state ...... that comfortably covers her living costs.
Since I always run out of money before payday, I’ve decided to apply for a/an ...... facility at my bank.
items.explanation: Borrow when account is empty → **overdraft** facility.

items.sentence: The letting agency requires a 20% ...... before handing over the keys to the apartment.
The court ordered the company to pay Mr Rivera £250,000 ...... for the injuries he sustained at work.

items.sentenceAfter: together, we can easily cover the cost of a weekend away.
items.originalSentence: If we all **gather / club / pool** together, we can easily cover the cost of a weekend away.
items.completedSentence: If we all club together, we can easily cover the cost of a weekend away.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "club" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "club" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "gather" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.gather: "gather" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("together, we can easily cover the cost of a weekend awa…").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: up a lot more enthusiasm if you want the committee to back your proposal.
items.originalSentence: You’ll need to **stir / drum / beat** up a lot more enthusiasm if you want the committee to back your proposal.
items.completedSentence: You’ll need to drum up a lot more enthusiasm if you want the committee to back your proposal.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "drum" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "drum" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "stir" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.stir: "stir" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("up a lot more enthusiasm if you want the committee to b…").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: up over 5,000 hours of commercial flying time.
items.originalSentence: The veteran pilot had **logged / clocked / counted** up over 5,000 hours of commercial flying time.
items.completedSentence: The veteran pilot had clocked up over 5,000 hours of commercial flying time.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "clocked" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "clocked" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "logged" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.logged: "logged" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("up over 5,000 hours of commercial flying time.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: up and we’re still only halfway through the project.
items.originalSentence: Our renovation costs are **rising / growing / mounting** up and we’re still only halfway through the project.
items.completedSentence: Our renovation costs are mounting up and we’re still only halfway through the project.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "mounting" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "mounting" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "rising" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.rising: "rising" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("up and we’re still only halfway through the project.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: the options up carefully before we commit to signing the contract.
items.originalSentence: Let’s **weigh / measure / size** the options up carefully before we commit to signing the contract.
items.completedSentence: Let’s size the options up carefully before we commit to signing the contract.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "size" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "size" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "weigh" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.weigh: "weigh" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("the options up carefully before we commit to signing th…").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: It seems like a large sum, but if you
items.sentenceAfter: it down into weekly amounts, it’s quite manageable.
items.originalSentence: It seems like a large sum, but if you **split / break / divide** it down into weekly amounts, it’s quite manageable.
items.completedSentence: It seems like a large sum, but if you break it down into weekly amounts, it’s quite manageable.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "break" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "break" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "split" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.split: "split" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("it down into weekly amounts, it’s quite manageable.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: She agreed to lend him the cash, but insisted he
items.sentenceAfter: her back before the month was out.
items.originalSentence: She agreed to lend him the cash, but insisted he **give / pay / return** her back before the month was out.
items.completedSentence: She agreed to lend him the cash, but insisted he pay her back before the month was out.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "pay" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "pay" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "give" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.give: "give" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("her back before the month was out.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: up properties along the new transport corridor.
items.originalSentence: Investors have been **snapping / buying / grabbing** up properties along the new transport corridor.
items.completedSentence: Investors have been buying up properties along the new transport corridor.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "buying" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "buying" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "snapping" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.snapping: "snapping" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("up properties along the new transport corridor.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: more seriously about your future career path.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "you thought" — it's high time + past simple.

wrongOptions.A: "to think" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.to think: "to think" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.B: "you think" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.you think: "you think" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.D: "you have thought" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.you have thought: "you have thought" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Eliminate options that break grammar first, then check which remaining word fits the meaning.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "you thought" is the only option that matches both meaning and register in this sentence.

items.sentenceAfter: anything to your parents yet, but I realise it’s difficult to keep it quiet.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "didn't say" — would rather + past.

wrongOptions.A: "not say" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "didn't say" is correct.
wrongOptions.not say: "not say" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "didn't say" is correct.
wrongOptions.B: "don't say" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "didn't say" is correct.
wrongOptions.don't say: "don't say" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "didn't say" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "hadn't said" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "didn't say" is correct.
wrongOptions.hadn't said: "hadn't said" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "didn't say" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Time expressions like for, since, already, and yet are strong present-perfect signals.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — look for markers like for, since, already, yet, or ever.

items.sentenceAfter: an official complaint about the quality of the service?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "to submit" — wish + infinitive.

wrongOptions.B: "submitting" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "to submit" is correct.
wrongOptions.submitting: "submitting" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "to submit" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "you submitted" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "to submit" is correct.
wrongOptions.you submitted: "you submitted" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "to submit" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "you to submit" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "to submit" is correct.
wrongOptions.you to submit: "you to submit" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "to submit" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Eliminate options that break grammar first, then check which remaining word fits the meaning.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Wish + past simple/past perfect expresses regret about the present or past.

items.sentenceBefore: If only people from our parents’ generation
items.sentenceAfter: the level of global travel that we enjoy today.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is regret about past possibility, so the correct answer is "could have had".

wrongOptions.B: "would have had" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "could have had" is correct.
wrongOptions.would have had: "would have had" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "could have had" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "would have" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "could have had" is correct.
wrongOptions.would have: "would have" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "could have had" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "have had" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "could have had" is correct.
wrongOptions.have had: "have had" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "could have had" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Eliminate options that break grammar first, then check which remaining word fits the meaning.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "could have had" is the only option that matches both meaning and register in this sentence.

items.sentenceAfter: to prepare a full financial report by tomorrow morning.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "didn't have" — wish about present.

wrongOptions.A: "doesn't have" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "didn't have" is correct.
wrongOptions.doesn't have: "doesn't have" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "didn't have" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "wouldn't have" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "didn't have" is correct.
wrongOptions.wouldn't have: "wouldn't have" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "didn't have" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "hadn't" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "didn't have" is correct.
wrongOptions.hadn't: "hadn't" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "didn't have" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Eliminate options that break grammar first, then check which remaining word fits the meaning.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Wish + past simple/past perfect expresses regret about the present or past.

items.sentenceAfter: any slower, we’d never have caught the last train home.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is inverted third conditional, so the correct answer is "Had the service been".

wrongOptions.A: "Were the service" does not fit the third conditional pattern (if + past perfect, would have + past participle).
wrongOptions.Were the service: "Were the service" does not fit the third conditional pattern (if + past perfect, would have + past participle).
wrongOptions.C: "Should the service be" does not fit the third conditional pattern (if + past perfect, would have + past participle).
wrongOptions.Should the service be: "Should the service be" does not fit the third conditional pattern (if + past perfect, would have + past participle).
wrongOptions.D: "Be the service" does not fit the third conditional pattern (if + past perfect, would have + past participle).
wrongOptions.Be the service: "Be the service" does not fit the third conditional pattern (if + past perfect, would have + past participle).

explanationContent.usefulTip: Name the conditional type first — that tells you which verb forms belong in each clause.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Third conditional: if + past perfect, would have + past participle — an unreal past situation and its past result.

items.sentenceBefore: I’d never lend money to a complete stranger unless they

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "showed" — hypothetical unless.

wrongOptions.A: "have shown" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "showed" is correct.
wrongOptions.have shown: "have shown" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "showed" is correct.
wrongOptions.B: "will show" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "showed" is correct.
wrongOptions.will show: "will show" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "showed" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "shows" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "showed" is correct.
wrongOptions.shows: "shows" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "showed" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Time expressions like for, since, already, and yet are strong present-perfect signals.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — look for markers like for, since, already, yet, or ever.

items.sentenceAfter: invented. Communication would be so much slower, wouldn’t it?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "hadn't been" — imagine + past perfect.

wrongOptions.A: "wasn't" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "hadn't been" is correct.
wrongOptions.wasn't: "wasn't" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "hadn't been" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "to not be" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "hadn't been" is correct.
wrongOptions.to not be: "to not be" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "hadn't been" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "to not have been" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "hadn't been" is correct.
wrongOptions.to not have been: "to not have been" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "hadn't been" is correct.
